mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 8 Series 840Ci engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 840Ci engine typically costs £2,200–£6,500 supply only depending on generation. M60-era engines sit at the lower end (£2,200–£5,500), while M62 units run £2,500–£6,500. Fitting adds £900–£2,000 depending on workshop location. A total replacement cost for a new BMW engine can fall within £7,500–£9,500+. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 8 Series 840Ci engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which of the two engine generations your 840Ci has. The M60 (1992–1996) is notorious for the Nikasil bore lining issue — high-sulfur fuel caused premature wear. Most affected engines were replaced under warranty. The M62 (1996–1999) uses Alusil liners and resolves this issue, but timing chain guide failure is a known problem. Both engines suffer from cooling system failures and oil leaks. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 8 Series 840Ci?

acceptedAnswer.text: Nikasil bore wear on the M60 (pre-1996) — compression loss, oil consumption, rough running. Timing chain guide failure on the M62 (post-1996) — rattling, 'slap' noise, plastic debris in oil. Cooling system failures on both engines — radiator leaks, water pump failure. Oil leaks from valve cover and timing cover gaskets.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 8 Series 840Ci engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 70% BMW scrap threshold: if your 840Ci is worth £6,000 and a recon engine is £4,000 (67%), it's borderline. If the vehicle is a tired £4,500 example, the same engine exceeds 70% — get a scrap valuation first. The 840Ci's classic car status and enthusiast following often justify replacement spend if the rest of the car is in good condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 8 Series 840Ci engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: 1992–1996 is M60B40 (4.0L V8), 1996–1999 is M62B44 (4.4L V8). The 840Ci was fitted to the E31 chassis. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the Nikasil bore wear, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The Nikasil issue is best resolved by replacing the engine with a later Alusil-block unit (£2,000–£5,000). A compression test is essential before buying any early 840Ci. If caught early, a rebuild may be possible, but replacement is often more cost-effective.

eraMap.title: The 840Ci THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: Nikasil lining is the critical weakness — verify block type
Alusil block resolves Nikasil — timing chain guides are main concern

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

rows.bestFor: No longer available — specialist reconditioning is the route

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect both M60 and M62 engine generations. See the Era Map above for era-specific pricing. A total replacement cost for a new BMW engine can fall within £7,500–£9,500+, though reconditioned units are significantly more affordable.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an 840Ci engine typically takes 14–20 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The V8's tight engine bay and complex electronics add significant labour time. Parts for the 8 Series can be hard to find and expensive.

cta.label: → Compare 8 Series 840Ci engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Nikasil Cylinder Liner Wear (M60B40 — 1992–1996)
problems.symptoms: Loss of compression, excessive oil consumption, black exhaust smoke, rough running, misfires, eventual engine failure.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, compression loss makes the engine undriveable.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: The M60's Nikasil cylinder lining is its fatal flaw. High-sulfur gasoline in the 1990s reacted with the coating, causing bore wear. BMW later switched to Alusil blocks, which are durable. A compression test is essential before buying any early 840Ci. If you experience oil consumption or rough running, have the engine inspected immediately. Most surviving engines have been replaced with Alusil units — verify your car's history.

problems.title: Timing Chain Guide Failure (M62B44 — 1996–1999)
problems.symptoms: Rattling or "slap" noise from the engine, excessive vibration, plastic particles in the oil, check engine light.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only if caught early; if the chain jumps, piston-valve contact can destroy the engine.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling, book an inspection today.

problems.recommendation: The M62's timing chain guides are a main failure point. The plastic guides become brittle with age and break, sending debris into the oil system. If the chain jumps timing, it can cause catastrophic engine damage. A timing chain kit for the M62 includes guides, tensioners, and chains. At the first sign of rattling, book an inspection immediately — preventative replacement is far cheaper than a full engine rebuild.

problems.title: Cooling System Failures (M60 & M62)
problems.symptoms: Sudden coolant leaks, frequent need to top up coolant, engine overheating, unstable temperature, audible bearing noise from water pump.
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–100,000 miles — age-related as plastic components become brittle.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; ignoring coolant warnings can lead to head gasket failure.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if the coolant warning comes on, pull over immediately.

problems.recommendation: The cooling system on both M60 and M62 engines relies on plastic components — radiator end tanks, hose flanges, and thermostat housings — that become brittle with age. The water pump impeller can also fail. Neglecting the cooling system can lead to catastrophic overheating. If you see any coolant warnings, have the system inspected immediately.

rows.whenItMakesSense: Replace with Alusil block — resolves permanently
Preventative — catches fault before catastrophic failure
Simple fix — do it immediately to avoid head gasket failure
Common maintenance — repair when symptoms appear
Full engine replacement required if bottom-end or valvetrain fails

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 840Ci? Check These First
buyIf: * Post-1996 M62 with Alusil block — Nikasil issue resolved
* M60 with documented Alusil replacement or confirmed healthy compression test
* Full service history with regular maintenance
* Timing chain guides replaced on M62 (especially over 80,000 miles)
avoidIf: * Pre-1996 M60 without compression test — Nikasil bore wear can be a £5,000 surprise
* Rattling or "slap" from the engine — timing chain guide failure on M62
* Coolant leaks or overheating — cooling system failure
* Poor service history — these engines need regular maintenance

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 840Ci is a classic grand tourer that deserves careful ownership. The early M60 V8 is a brilliant engine but the Nikasil issue is a critical weak point — verify the block type before purchase. The later M62 is more reliable but timing chain guides are a known failure point. Both engines suffer from ageing cooling systems and oil leaks. With the 70% BMW scrap threshold, a £4,000 recon on a £6,000 840Ci is 67% — borderline. On a sub-£5,000 example, think carefully. The 840Ci is considered the better overall value versus the V12 850Ci.

cta.label: → Compare 8 Series 840Ci engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 8 Series 840Ci (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Compression Test — Essential on M60 (pre-1996) — Nikasil bore wear is a known issue
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling or "slap" — M62 timing chain guide warning
* Service History — Including which engine era (M60/M62) and confirmed maintenance
* Coolant Check — Check level and condition — cooling system failures are common
* Oil Leaks — Check around valve covers and timing covers — age-related leaks
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles? Could indicate Nikasil wear on M60
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures

items.question: Craft Section 9 (Trust + Final CTA) with 3 trust points that reflect the classic car nature and specific engine issues of the 840Ci (Nikasil, timing chain guides), keeping the "Consultative" mode from Check A.
Generate the Meta Title and Description with appropriate keywords and length.
Create the Open Graph and Twitter Card tags consistent with the meta info.

Build the complex JSON-LD schema. This is the most critical part. The schema must include BreadcrumbList, ItemList for the Era Map (exactly matching the two rows I created), ItemList for Engine Codes (matching M60B40 and M62B44), FAQPage with all six questions, Organization, Service (with price range spanning from the lowest used M60 to highest M62/classic rebuild price), Dataset, WebPage, and importantly, a conditional AggregateRating (which I'll omit as per standard practice for a new site without genuine reviews).

I must also write the Production Note, confirming the schema list matches the Era Map, explaining the price range source, and detailing the Authority Model verification sources used for the reliability ratings I assigned in Part 1.
items.answer: The user's deep need is for a complete, publication-ready page that passes all the quality assurance checks in the Handover Document, especially the ones added in v2.0 about the Era Map, Data Notes, and schema consistency. The assistant's response needs to be flawlessly structured, matching the format of all previous "Part 2" responses in the history, to demonstrate full compliance with the system.
